stem: The parasternal long-axis (PLAX) view displays which structures?
options: Aortic valve, mitral valve, left ventricle, left atrium, aortic root
rationale: PLAX shows: RV (anterior), IVS, LV cavity, mitral valve, aortic valve/root, LA, and descending aorta posterior to LA.

stem: The apical four-chamber view is obtained by placing the transducer at:
options: The cardiac apex with the beam directed toward the right shoulder
The second intercostal space at the right sternal border
rationale: A4C view: transducer at apex, beam toward right shoulder. Shows all four chambers, both AV valves, interatrial and interventricular septa.

stem: In the parasternal short-axis view at the aortic valve level, the normal aortic valve appears as:
options: Three cusps forming a 'Mercedes-Benz' or inverted Y pattern during diastole
Two cusps forming a fish-mouth pattern
rationale: Normal aortic valve has three cusps (right coronary, left coronary, non-coronary) forming the classic three-pointed star pattern in diastole.

stem: The subcostal (subxiphoid) view is particularly useful in:
options: Patients with hyperinflated lungs (COPD/emphysema) where parasternal windows are poor
rationale: Subcostal approach avoids lung interference and is valuable in patients with COPD/emphysema, post-cardiac surgery, or when standard windows are inadequate.

stem: M-mode echocardiography provides:
options: High temporal resolution for measuring chamber dimensions and valve motion
rationale: M-mode provides excellent temporal resolution (1000+ lines/sec vs 30-60 fps for 2D) for precise timing of cardiac events and accurate measurements of wall thickness, chamber size, and valve motion.

stem: Continuous wave (CW) Doppler is preferred over pulsed wave (PW) for:
options: Measuring high-velocity jets like aortic stenosis because CW has no Nyquist limit
Localizing the exact depth of a flow signal
rationale: CW Doppler can measure unlimited velocities (no aliasing) but cannot localize the depth. PW provides range resolution but is limited by the Nyquist limit.

stem: The E/A ratio in mitral inflow Doppler assesses:
rationale: E wave (early passive filling) and A wave (atrial contraction) ratio evaluates LV diastolic function. Normal E/A >1. Reversed ratio (E/A <1) suggests impaired relaxation.

stem: In aortic stenosis, CW Doppler across the aortic valve shows:
options: Increased peak velocity and mean gradient
rationale: Severe AS: peak velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg, valve area <1.0 cm². CW Doppler measures peak velocity to calculate gradients using modified Bernoulli.

stem: The modified Bernoulli equation for pressure gradient is:
rationale: Modified Bernoulli: ΔP = 4V² (simplified from full equation). Converts peak velocity (m/s) to pressure gradient (mmHg). Essential for valve gradient calculations.

stem: Tissue Doppler imaging (TDI) measures:
options: Myocardial velocities at the mitral annulus to assess diastolic function
rationale: TDI measures low-velocity, high-amplitude signals from myocardial motion. E' (e-prime) at the mitral annulus is a key measure of diastolic function. E/e' estimates filling pressures.

stem: Severe aortic stenosis is characterized by:
options: Peak velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg, valve area <1.0 cm²
rationale: Severe AS criteria: peak velocity >4 m/s, mean gradient >40 mmHg, AVA <1.0 cm² (or indexed <0.6 cm²/m²). Symptoms include angina, syncope, heart failure.

stem: The continuity equation is used to calculate:
rationale: Continuity equation: AVA = (LVOT area × LVOT VTI) / AV VTI. Based on conservation of mass - flow through LVOT equals flow through aortic valve.

stem: Low-flow, low-gradient aortic stenosis with reduced EF requires:
options: Dobutamine stress echo to differentiate true severe AS from pseudo-severe AS
rationale: Low-flow, low-gradient AS with reduced EF: dobutamine stress determines if valve opens more (pseudo-severe) or gradient increases with fixed small area (true severe AS).

stem: Bicuspid aortic valve is associated with:
options: Aortopathy with risk of ascending aortic dilation and dissection
rationale: Bicuspid AV (1-2% of population) associated with ascending aortic dilation, coarctation, and accelerated valve degeneration leading to earlier AS or AR.

stem: Aortic valve sclerosis differs from stenosis in that:
options: Sclerosis shows calcification/thickening without significant obstruction (peak velocity <2.5 m/s)
rationale: Sclerosis: thickened/calcified valve without hemodynamically significant obstruction (Vmax <2.5 m/s). Progresses to stenosis in ~16% over 2-4 years.

stem: Severe mitral regurgitation jet characteristics on color Doppler include:
options: Vena contracta width ≥7mm, large eccentric jet reaching posterior LA wall
rationale: Severe MR: vena contracta ≥7mm, EROA ≥0.4 cm², regurgitant volume ≥60 mL, regurgitant fraction ≥50%. Large jets may be central or eccentric (Coanda effect).

stem: The PISA (proximal isovelocity surface area) method is used to:
options: Quantify the effective regurgitant orifice area and regurgitant volume
rationale: PISA uses the flow convergence zone proximal to the regurgitant orifice to calculate EROA and regurgitant volume. Adjust baseline to optimize hemisphere visualization.

stem: Mitral valve prolapse is diagnosed when:
options: One or both mitral leaflets displace ≥2mm above the annular plane in PLAX view
The valve is thickened without displacement
rationale: MVP: systolic displacement of one or both leaflets ≥2mm above the mitral annular plane in parasternal long-axis view. Significant when >5mm (classic/myxomatous).

stem: Flail mitral leaflet is characterized by:
options: A leaflet tip that moves into the LA during systole with ruptured chordae
rationale: Flail leaflet: ruptured chordae tendineae cause the leaflet tip to prolapse into the LA during systole. Results in severe eccentric MR jet directed away from the flail leaflet.

stem: Dilated cardiomyopathy echo findings include:
options: LV dilation with globally reduced wall motion and decreased EF
Small LV cavity with thickened walls
Normal LV size with regional wall motion abnormality
rationale: DCM: four-chamber dilation (especially LV), globally decreased contractility, reduced EF (<40%), mitral/tricuspid regurgitation, possible LV thrombus.

stem: The echocardiographic feature that distinguishes hypertrophic cardiomyopathy from hypertensive heart disease is:
options: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy (septum/posterior wall ratio >1.3) and SAM of the mitral valve
rationale: HCM shows asymmetric hypertrophy (typically involving the septum), systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve, and dynamic LVOT obstruction in obstructive HCM.

stem: LVOT obstruction in hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) produces:
options: A late-peaking, dagger-shaped CW Doppler signal in the LVOT
An early-peaking signal like aortic stenosis
rationale: HOCM LVOT obstruction: dynamic obstruction produces a late-peaking (unlike AS which is symmetric), dagger-shaped Doppler signal. Gradient increases with Valsalva, standing, dehydration.

stem: Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve in HCM:
options: Causes LVOT obstruction and secondary mitral regurgitation
rationale: SAM: the anterior mitral leaflet is drawn toward the septum during systole (Venturi effect), causing LVOT obstruction and posteriorly-directed MR.

stem: Provocative maneuvers that increase LVOT gradient in HOCM include:
options: Valsalva maneuver, standing, dehydration, and post-PVC beats
rationale: Maneuvers that decrease preload or afterload or increase contractility worsen obstruction: Valsalva, standing, dehydration, post-PVC augmentation, exercise.

stem: An atrial septal defect (ASD) on echo shows:
options: Right heart volume overload with RA and RV dilation and paradoxical septal motion
rationale: ASD causes left-to-right shunt leading to RA/RV volume overload and dilation. The septum moves paradoxically (flattens/bows toward LV in diastole). May see contrast bubble crossing.

stem: The most common type of ASD is:
rationale: Secundum ASD (75-80% of cases) occurs at the fossa ovalis (central septum). Primum ASD is at the inferior septum near AV valves. Sinus venosus is near the SVC.

stem: Qp/Qs ratio >1.5 in an ASD indicates:
options: Significant left-to-right shunt that may warrant closure
rationale: Qp/Qs (pulmonary-to-systemic flow ratio) >1.5 indicates hemodynamically significant shunt. This is a criterion for closure consideration.

stem: A restrictive VSD is characterized by:
options: Small defect with high-velocity jet and large pressure gradient between ventricles
rationale: Restrictive VSD: small defect, high velocity jet (4-5 m/s), large interventricular gradient. Non-restrictive: large defect, low velocity, equalized pressures.

stem: Membranous (perimembranous) VSDs are located:
options: In the membranous septum below the aortic valve, best seen in PLAX and A5C views
At the inlet septum near the tricuspid valve
In the outlet septum near the pulmonary valve
rationale: Membranous VSDs (80% of all VSDs) are in the membranous septum, adjacent to the aortic valve. Best visualized in PLAX, A5C, and subcostal views.

stem: The biplane Simpson's (method of discs) calculates LVEF from:
options: Apical 4-chamber and 2-chamber views by tracing endocardial borders at end-diastole and end-systole
rationale: Simpson's biplane: trace LV endocardium in A4C and A2C at end-diastole and end-systole. EDV and ESV are calculated from stacked discs. EF = (EDV-ESV)/EDV × 100%.

stem: Normal left ventricular ejection fraction is:
rationale: Normal LVEF is 55-70%. Mildly reduced: 41-54%. Moderately reduced: 30-40%. Severely reduced: <30%.

stem: Cardiac output is calculated as:
rationale: CO = SV × HR. Stroke volume = EDV - ESV, or calculated from LVOT area × LVOT VTI. Normal CO: 4-8 L/min.

stem: Stroke volume by Doppler is calculated as:
rationale: SV = LVOT CSA × LVOT VTI. CSA = π(d/2)² where d is LVOT diameter. VTI is the velocity-time integral of PW Doppler in the LVOT.

stem: Pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP) is estimated from:
options: Peak TR velocity using modified Bernoulli plus estimated RAP
rationale: PASP = 4(V_TR)² + RAP. TR jet velocity provides the RV-RA gradient. Adding estimated RAP (from IVC size/collapsibility) gives PASP.

stem: IVC diameter <2.1cm with >50% inspiratory collapse suggests RAP of:
rationale: IVC <2.1cm with >50% collapse = normal RAP (0-5 mmHg, use 3). IVC >2.1cm with <50% collapse = elevated RAP (10-20, use 15). Intermediate = 8 mmHg.

stem: Elevated pulmonary pressure is suggested when PASP exceeds:
rationale: Normal PASP is 15-30 mmHg. Mild PH: 35-50 mmHg. Moderate: 50-70 mmHg. Severe: >70 mmHg. Clinical threshold typically 35-40 mmHg.

stem: A large pericardial effusion on echo is defined as:
rationale: Small: <10mm. Moderate: 10-20mm. Large: >20mm. Size correlates with volume: small <100mL, moderate 100-500mL, large >500mL.

stem: Cardiac tamponade is diagnosed by echo findings of:
options: RA systolic collapse, RV diastolic collapse, respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid inflow, dilated IVC
rationale: Tamponade signs: RA collapse in late diastole/early systole, RV diastolic collapse, exaggerated respiratory variation (>25% mitral, >40% tricuspid), dilated non-collapsing IVC.

stem: The echocardiographic swinging heart pattern is seen with:
options: Large pericardial effusion causing the heart to swing freely within the fluid
rationale: Swinging heart: with large effusions, the heart oscillates freely within the pericardial fluid, causing electrical alternans on ECG.

stem: Constrictive pericarditis differs from restrictive cardiomyopathy by:
options: Septal bounce (interventricular dependence) with exaggerated respiratory variation in constriction
Elevated filling pressures in both conditions equally
Absence of respiratory variation in constriction
rationale: Constriction: septal bounce, enhanced ventricular interdependence, >25% respiratory variation in mitral inflow, thickened/calcified pericardium. Restriction: no bounce, <10% variation.

stem: A thickened pericardium (>4mm) is best assessed by:
options: CT or MRI, as echo may underestimate thickness
rationale: While echo can suggest thickening, CT and MRI are more accurate for measuring pericardial thickness. Normal pericardium is 1-2mm.

stem: A comprehensive transthoracic echocardiogram should include:
options: 2D imaging of all standard views, M-mode, color Doppler, spectral Doppler (PW and CW) of all valves
rationale: Comprehensive TTE per ASE guidelines: 2D imaging (PLAX, PSAX, A4C, A2C, A3C/A5C, subcostal, suprasternal), M-mode, color flow of all valves, PW/CW Doppler, tissue Doppler.

stem: The LVOT diameter for stroke volume calculation is measured in:
options: Parasternal long-axis view during mid-systole, inner edge to inner edge
Short-axis view at papillary muscle level
rationale: LVOT diameter is measured in PLAX view, mid-systole, inner edge to inner edge at the aortic annulus level. Accuracy is critical as it is squared in the area calculation.

stem: Quality assurance in the echo lab includes:
options: Image quality review, correlation with surgical/catheterization findings, inter-reader variability assessment
Counting the number of studies performed
rationale: Echo lab QA includes image quality review, measurement reproducibility, correlation with gold standard findings, continuing education, and accreditation requirements (IAC).
